item.title: Police Officer found dead in Sigatoka
item.description: The body of a man found in Sigatoka has been identified as a police officer. The Fiji Police Force confirmed the victim's identity today, stating that the circumstances surrounding the officer's death remain under investigation.
item.content:encoded:
The body of a man found in Sigatoka has been identified as a police officer.

The Fiji Police Force confirmed the victim's identity today, stating that the circumstances surrounding the officer's death remain under investigation.

Police says a post-mortem examination will be conducted to determine the cause of death, with further details to be released as investigations progress.

Commissioner of Police Rusiate Tudravu has extended the organisation's deepest sympathies to the officer's family during this difficult time.

item.title: Those concerned over school preaching video should report it to authorities - Rabuka
item.description: People concerned about the viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should report the matter to the appropriate authorities rather than the Prime Minister. While responding to questions about the incident, Rabuka says those who believe there was any wrongdoing should take their concerns to a gencies such as the Police, the Online Safety Commissi
item.content:encoded:
People concerned about the viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should report the matter to the appropriate authorities rather than the Prime Minister.

While responding to questions about the incident, Rabuka says those who believe there was any wrongdoing should take their concerns to a gencies such as the Police, the Online Safety Commission and other relevant authorities, which would then deal with the matter through the proper government processes.

Insert: PM - Take it to the authorities July 23

Rabuka says while the Government establishes laws and policies through Parliament, it is the responsibility of relevant departments and agencies to enforce them.

When asked whether the incident raised constitutional issues, Rabuka questioned the link, saying the focus should remain on whether any laws had been broken.

Meanwhile, a video circulating on social media that appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students during a school session.

In the footage, Osaslugo appears to single out a student identified as Muslim before telling him that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The video has attracted widespread criticism online, with many expressing concern that the child was publicly singled out because of his religion and subjected to comments that could be viewed as discriminatory and inappropriate in a school setting.

Questions have also been raised about how the preacher was allowed to address students during school hours and whether the school had approved the session.

The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has since called on the Ministry of Education to investigate the incident promptly, saying any situation where a child feels targeted, pressured, humiliated or excluded because of their religion raises significant human rights and child protection concerns.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: FCCC warns businesses against limiting sugar purchases
item.description: Businesses are being warned they could face enforcement action if they restrict consumers from buying the quantity of sugar they need. This was highlighted by the FCCC, saying retailers and traders must not impose purchase limits or ration the amount of sugar a customer can buy , as doing so may breach Section 87(I) of the FCCC Act 2010.
item.content:encoded:
Businesses are being warned they could face enforcement action if they restrict consumers from buying the quantity of sugar they need.

This was highlighted by the FCCC, saying retailers and traders must not impose purchase limits or ration the amount of sugar a customer can buy , as doing so may breach Section 87(I) of the FCCC Act 2010.

The warning comes as the FCCC's enforcement officers continue inspections across the country to assess market conditions relating to the rationing and conditional selling of sugar at the retail level.

The FCCC says its teams are checking bulk storage facilities, verifying purchase orders, reviewing distribution patterns and monitoring supply chain movements, including the frequency of sugar deliveries and replenishment schedules.

The teams are also engaging with retailers, traders and businesses to establish expected stock availability and how long current and anticipated sugar supplies are expected to remain on shelves.

The Commission says while genuine supply disruptions can happen, businesses cannot introduce their own rationing measures with retailers are not permitted to advertise or sell sugar with conditions that limit the quantity a customer can purchase.

Where stock is available and a consumer requests a quantity of sugar while offering the correct payment, the trader must not refuse or fail to supply the requested amount.

Businesses are also reminded that conditional selling is prohibited.

This includes situations where a product is only supplied if a customer buys another product or spends a minimum amount.

The FCCC says compulsory tie-in sales or bundling arrangements that force consumers to purchase additional goods may also breach Section 87(E) of the FCCC Act 2010.

The Commission says sugar remains a price-controlled item, and retail prices must be assessed, reviewed and approved by the FCCC under the Fijian Competition and Consumer Commission (Price Control) (Food Item Prices) Order 2024.

It says any trader importing sugar must submit an application to the FCCC before selling the product to consumers, in accordance with Section 44 of the FCCC Act 2010.

The FCCC says any breaches of the Act may be treated as a statutory offence and could result in formal investigations and enforcement action.

Businesses have been advised to review their current sales practices, including policies on bulk purchases and promotional bundles, and ensure frontline staff are properly trained to avoid non-compliance.

Consumers who experience overcharging or unfair trading practices can report these matters to the FCCC by calling 8921991 or emailing helpdesk@fccc.gov.fj .

Consumers can also contact the FCCC's Western Division office on 8911623, Sigatoka residents on 9982437, or the Northern Division office on 8911624.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Former Company Group CEO remanded after being convicted of indecent assault against employee
item.description: Mansi Chand , Vijay Narayan · 23/07/2026 A former Chief Executive Officer of a major company has been remanded after being convicted of two counts of indecent assault against his employee.
item.content:encoded:
Mansi Chand , Vijay Narayan · 23/07/2026

A former Chief Executive Officer of a major company has been remanded after being convicted of two counts of indecent assault against his employee.

His name suppression stands in this case for now based on a separate court order.

In this case, Justice Waleen George has found the former CEO guilty on both charges.

The offences were committed on the 25th of June and the 13th of September 2019.

During the trial, the victim gave evidence that around May 2019, she had an appointment at the former CEO's office for a job.

She said that during the appointment, the then CEO asked her about being engaged too young and when she stood up to leave, he hugged her and sniffed her hair and neck for five seconds before she left.

She said that she went blank and didn't know what happened.

The former employee mentioned that a few days later she had an interview and started working for the company after which the incident occurred again.

The judgement was delivered two years after the trial concluded.

The sentence hearing will be held on the 12th of next month.

In the first case, he was charged with one count of indecent assault, where it was alleged that on the 15th of May 2019, the former CEO indecently assaulted a woman by hugging her and sniffing and kissing her neck whilst rubbing her back.

On 1st November 2021, the court found the man guilty as charged.

He was then sentenced to 14 months imprisonment, suspended for 2 years.

In that case, the former CEO appeal the conviction and sentence, and said there was no reliable or credible evidence against him, the complainant was the most evasive and untruthful witness with no credibility, the complainant had become good friends with the former CEO and had pictures taken with him and also sent him emails and was previously hugged by the man without any complaint, and the woman's complaint was 7 months after the alleged incident and was in similar terms as reported by Lynda Tabuya in her Facebook post of 6th December 2019.

His appeal against conviction in that case was allowed by the court.

item.title: Magistrate to rule on Kamikamica's no case to answer application on August 3rd
item.description: Magistrate Charles Ratakele will rule on 3rd August in the no case to answer application for former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica's perjury trial in the Suva Magistrates Court. This is after both sides filed their closing submissions.
item.content:encoded:
Magistrate Charles Ratakele will rule on 3rd August in the no case to answer application for former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica's perjury trial in the Suva Magistrates Court.

This is after both sides filed their closing submissions.

Defence lawyer Wylie Clarke filed for a no case to answer yesterday after FICAC had closed their case.

Kamikamica is charged with perjury and giving false statement to a public servant

FICAC had called two witnesses - Janet Mason who assisted the COI as well as Government MP Kalaveti Ravu.

item.title: From balancing family life to becoming FNU's two times 2025 Research Innovator awardee
item.description: Dr Prasad received the Research Innovation Excellence Award and was also named 2025 Research Innovator of the Year at the FNU Research and Innovation Excellence Awards. Speaking to fijivillage News, Dr Prasad says the recognition is the result of years of hard work, perseverance and the support of the people around her.
item.content:encoded:
Dr Prasad received the Research Innovation Excellence Award and was also named 2025 Research Innovator of the Year at the FNU Research and Innovation Excellence Awards.

Speaking to fijivillage News, Dr Prasad says the recognition is the result of years of hard work, perseverance and the support of the people around her.

She says her current research focuses on improving access to clean cooking energy for households by working with communities, women's groups, non-government organisations, the private sector and fellow researchers to develop practical solutions that improve people's lives.

Dr Prasad says her passion for community-based research began during her PhD after her supervisor encouraged her to ensure her work gave back to society.

She says one of the biggest challenges has been balancing research, teaching and family responsibilities.

Dr Prasad says her mother has played a vital role by caring for her children while she attended workshops and carried out fieldwork, while her husband has supported her by reviewing her research before it was published.

She also acknowledged the support of her supervisors, colleagues and the FNU Research Office, saying research is never an individual effort but the result of teamwork and collaboration.

Dr Prasad hopes her journey will inspire more women and young researchers to pursue research that makes a real difference in people's lives and communities.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Human Rights Commission calls for investigation into school preaching incident
item.description: The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called on the Ministry of Education to promptly investigate allegations that a student was singled out because of his religion during a religious session led by an external visitor at a prominent secondary school. Responding to questions from fijivillage News, Commission Director Loukinikini Lewaravu says the reports and video footage ci
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called on the Ministry of Education to promptly investigate allegations that a student was singled out because of his religion during a religious session led by an external visitor at a prominent secondary school.

Responding to questions from fijivillage News, Commission Director Loukinikini Lewaravu says the reports and video footage circulating on social media raised serious human rights and child protection concerns, although the facts of the matter are still being established.

Lewaravu says any situation in which a child feels targeted, pressured, humiliated or excluded because of their religion, beliefs, identity or background warrants careful examination.

She says every child has the right to learn in a safe, respectful and inclusive environment, adding that schools should be places where all students feel valued regardless of their religion, ethnicity, background or beliefs.

Lewaravu stresses that no child should experience discrimination, intimidation, coercion or treatment that undermines their wellbeing, confidence, sense of belonging or educational experience.

She says child safeguarding extends beyond protecting children from physical harm and also includes protection from emotional and psychological harm, discrimination, bullying, intimidation, inappropriate behaviour, harmful practices and any form of pressure relating to personal beliefs, religion, culture or identity.

The Commission is reminding schools and other duty bearers that Section 22 of the 2013 Constitution guarantees freedom of religion, conscience and belief, and provides that a child attending an educational institution cannot be required to receive religious instruction or participate in religious observances relating to a religion that is not their own without the consent of a parent or lawful guardian.

Lewaravu also highlighted Section 41 of the Constitution, which states that the best interests of the child must be the primary consideration in every matter concerning the child, including decisions and activities within the school environment .

The Commission is recommending that the Ministry of Education review policies governing external visitors and speakers to ensure appropriate vetting, supervision and child safeguarding standards are consistently applied across all schools.

They have also recommended that schools strengthen child safeguarding policies, establish clear codes of conduct for external visitors, including religious leaders, provide regular training on child rights and religious diversity, and ensure children and parents have safe and accessible avenues to raise concerns.

Lewaravu says Fiji's cultural and religious diversity is one of the country's greatest strengths, and schools play a vital role in fostering mutual respect, understanding and social cohesion.

The Commission says it stands ready to work with the Ministry of Education and other stakeholders to strengthen child safeguarding measures and ensure every child can learn in an environment that protects their rights, dignity and wellbeing.

Meanwhile, the Commission's statement follows public concern over a video circulating on social media that appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka during school hours.

In the footage, Osaslugo appears to single out a student identified as Muslim and tells him that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The video has drawn widespread criticism online, with many questioning whether the child was publicly singled out because of his religion and whether the school had approved the session.
